coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as dual inhibitors of tau and amyloid-beta aggregation for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease

coredata.dc:description:
Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common form of senile dementia, presenting a significant challenge for the development of effective treatments. AD is characterized by extracellular amyloid plaques and intraneuronal neurofibrillary tangles. Therefore, targeting both hallmarks through inhibition of amyloid beta (Aβ) and tau aggregation presents a promising approach for drug development. Carbon dots (CD), with their high biocompatibility, minimal cytotoxicity, and blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability, have emerged as promising drug nanocarriers. Congo red, an azo dye, has gathered significant attention for inhibiting amyloid-beta and tau aggregation. However, Congo red's inability to cross the BBB limits its potential to be used as a drug candidate for central nervous system (CNS) diseases. Furthermore, current studies only focus on using Congo red to target single disease hallmarks, without investigating dual inhibition capabilities. In this study, we synthesized Congo red-derived CD (CRCD) by using Congo red and citric acid as precursors, resulting in three variants, CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3, based on different mass ratios of precursors. CRCD2 and CRCD3 exhibited sustained low cytotoxicity, and CRCD3 demonstrated the ability to traverse the BBB in a zebrafish model. Moreover, thioflavin T (ThT) aggregation assays and AFM imaging revealed CRCD as potent inhibitors against both tau and Aβ aggregation. Notably, CRCD1 emerged as the most robust inhibitor, displaying IC50 values of 0.2 ± 0.1 and 2.1 ± 0.5 µg/mL against tau and Aβ aggregation, respectively. Our findings underscore the dual inhibitory role of CRCD against tau and Aβ aggregation, showcasing effective BBB penetration and positioning CRCD as potential nanodrugs and nanocarriers for the CNS. Hence, CRCD-based compounds represent a promising candidate in the realm of multi-functional AD therapeutics, offering an innovative formulation component for future developments in this area.
Statement of significance This article reports Congo red-derived carbon dots (CRCD) as dual inhibitors of tau and amyloid-beta (Aβ) aggregation for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease (AD). The CRCD are biocompatible and show strong fluorescence, high stability, the ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, and the function of addressing two major pathological features of AD.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV/vis absorption spectrum of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 aqueous solutions. (B-D) Fluorescence emission spectra of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 in aqueous medium, respectively.
FTIR spectrum of CRCD1 (black), CRCD2 (red) and CRCD3 (green). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TGA spectra of (A) CRCD1, (B) CRCD2 and (C) CRCD3; DTG spectra of (D) CRCD1, (E) CRCD2 and (F) CRCD3.

XPS spectra of C 1s of (A) CRCD1, (B) CRCD2, (C) CRCD3; O 1s of (D) CRCD1, (E) CRCD2, (F) CRCD3; N 1s of (G) CRCD1, (H) CRCD2, (I) CRCD3; S 2p of (J) CRCD1, (K) CRCD2 and (L) CRCD3. XPS background was corrected using a Shirley line-shape type function.
Mass spectra of (A) CRCD1; (B) CRCD2 and (C) CRCD3. All samples were dispersed in DI water at a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL.
AFM images with size distribution of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3.

Cytotoxicity measurements of CRCD1, CRCD2, CRCD3 and Congo red at different concentrations that were applied to (A) HeLa and (B) HEK 293 cells and incubated for 72 hrs. The cell viability of the control group was shown as 100 %. Data are exhibited as the mean ± STD. Statistical significance was calculated using a t-test for 100 µg/mL condition in comparison with the 0 µg/mL control group and indicated as no significance (ns) (p-value ≥ 0.05) and ** (p-value ≤ 0.01). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ThT fluorescence data demonstrating (A) dose dependence of all three CRCD on tau aggregation and (B) dose dependence of all three CRCD on amyloid-beta aggregation. Statistical significance was calculated using a t-test for all three in comparison with the uninhibited control and indicated as * (p-value ≤ 0.05), ** (p-value ≤ 0.01), and *** (p-value ≤ 0.001).

Tau aggregation can be visualized via Atomic Force Microscopy, demonstrated above. Uninhibited tau can be seen farthest to the left with extensive fibril formation compared with the image on the far right with a 143 µg/mL of CRCD1, demonstrating minimal aggregation and confirming the presence of CRCD1.

Tau aggregation can be visualized via Transmission Electron Microscopy, demonstrated above. Uninhibited tau can be seen farthest to the right with extensive fibril formation compared with the image on the far left with 143 µg/mL (240 µM) CRCD1, demonstrating minimal aggregation and confirming the presence of CRCD1.

The IC50 values and standard deviations calculated from the ThT fluorescence of four replicates suggests CRCD1 is most effective at inhibiting tau and Aβ42 aggregation, seemingly more effective against tau (A) than Aβ42 (B). Statistical significance was calculated using a t-test for all three in comparison with the uninhibited control and indicated as * (p-value ≤ 0.05), ** (p-value ≤ 0.01), and *** (p-value ≤ 0.001).
Simplified schematic interpretation of CRCD interference with β-sheet aggregation.

Confocal images of zebrafish larvae injected with CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 aqueous solution (30 mg/mL) in contrast to the control group. The red arrow indicates the central canal of spinal cord of zebrafish. Each picture was reproduced with at least six larvae. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common form of senile dementia, presenting a significant challenge for the development of effective treatments. AD is characterized by extracellular amyloid plaques and intraneuronal neurofibrillary tangles. Therefore, targeting both hallmarks through inhibition of amyloid beta (Aβ) and tau aggregation presents a promising approach for drug development. Carbon dots (CD), with their high biocompatibility, minimal cytotoxicity, and blood-brain barrier (BBB) permeability, have emerged as promising drug nanocarriers. Congo red, an azo dye, has gathered significant attention for inhibiting amyloid-beta and tau aggregation. However, Congo red's inability to cross the BBB limits its potential to be used as a drug candidate for central nervous system (CNS) diseases. Furthermore, current studies only focus on using Congo red to target single disease hallmarks, without investigating dual inhibition capabilities. In this study, we synthesized Congo red-derived CD (CRCD) by using Congo red and citric acid as precursors, resulting in three variants, CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3, based on different mass ratios of precursors. CRCD2 and CRCD3 exhibited sustained low cytotoxicity, and CRCD3 demonstrated the ability to traverse the BBB in a zebrafish model. Moreover, thioflavin T (ThT) aggregation assays and AFM imaging revealed CRCD as potent inhibitors against both tau and Aβ aggregation. Notably, CRCD1 emerged as the most robust inhibitor, displaying IC50 values of 0.2 ± 0.1 and 2.1 ± 0.5 µg/mL against tau and Aβ aggregation, respectively. Our findings underscore the dual inhibitory role of CRCD against tau and Aβ aggregation, showcasing effective BBB penetration and positioning CRCD as potential nanodrugs and nanocarriers for the CNS. Hence, CRCD-based compounds represent a promising candidate in the realm of multi-functional AD therapeutics, offering an innovative formulation component for future developments in this area.

This article reports Congo red-derived carbon dots (CRCD) as dual inhibitors of tau and amyloid-beta (Aβ) aggregation for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease (AD). The CRCD are biocompatible and show strong fluorescence, high stability, the ability to cross the blood-brain barrier, and the function of addressing two major pathological features of AD.

Alzheimer’s disease is characterized by extracellular amyloid plaques and intraneuronal neurofibrillary tangles. In this study, we synthesized Congo red-derived carbon dots (CRCD) by using Congo red and citric acid as precursors. By optimizing the mass ratio of the precursors, CRCD exhibited the ability to traverse the blood-brain barrier and can function as dual inhibitors against both tau and amyloid-beta aggregation. CRCD-based compounds represent a promising candidate in the realm of multi-functional therapeutics for Alzheimer’s disease.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD) stands as the leading cause of dementia among the elderly population [1]. AD is a neurodegenerative disease afflicting an estimated 30 million people worldwide and the sixth leading cause of death in the USA [2]. Several AD drugs have been approved by the U. S. Food & Drug Administration (FDA) [3], but only the recently released Lecanumab and Donanemab, anti-amyloid antibodies, could significantly slow the progression of the disease [4]. In 2018, the U.S. National Institute on Aging (NIA) and the Alzheimer's Association proposed the ATN frameworks for the definition and diagnosis of AD, where A stands for amyloid deposition via amyloid beta (Aβ)-aggregation, T for tau pathology such as neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs), and N for neurodegeneration [1,5,6]. Amyloid plaques, a primary pathological feature of AD, consist predominantly of Aβ-peptide aggregates [7]. In tandem with amyloid plaque formation, the abnormal hyperphosphorylation of tau protein leads to the development of paired helical filaments (PHF), a characteristic component of neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs) [8]. These intracellular accumulations disrupt neuronal communication and contribute to neuronal death, further exacerbating cognitive decline in AD patients.

For many years the amyloid cascade hypothesis revolved around the notion that abnormal Aβ accumulation leads to toxic Aβ aggregates, which initiates the pathological process ultimately resulting in dementia. Aβ aggregates can also potentiate tau aggregation and the formation of tau pathology [9]. The vast majority of AD drug development initiatives concentrate on addressing either Aβ or tau pathology, selectively targeting one of these facets. Specifically, Aβ plaques represent a prominent hallmark of AD, prompting numerous investigations into inhibitors which has resulted in various experimental drugs that target different stages of the amyloid fibrillation process [10]. Naturally occurring products, such as epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) derived from green tea, exhibit inhibitory effects on Aβ fibrillogenesis by directly binding to the intrinsically disordered Aβ peptides and impeding the formation of aggregation intermediates [11,12]. Furthermore, alternately truncated Aβ also demonstrates efficacy in breaking β-sheet formations, thereby suppressing fibrillation [13]. Several small molecules, including dobutamine, resveratrol, and different polyphenols, form colloidal nanoparticles ranging from 50 to 600 nm, capable of nonspecifically inhibiting the formation of Aβ plaques [14]. Numerous other aggregation inhibitors have been investigated, such as tau antibodies PHF1 and MC1, which target neurofibrillary tangles to mitigate tau pathology [15]. Years of study suggest the pathogenesis of AD is intricate, influenced by a multitude of environmental and genetic factors. While ongoing drug discovery endeavors primarily focus on targeting single factors, notably Aβ [16], there is an established need to advance therapeutic strategies that engage multiple targets. In this context, addressing Aβ and tau aggregation concurrently emerges as a critical avenue for the development of more effective AD therapies with disease-modifying potential.

Congo red, an azo dye, has garnered significant attention as one of the most extensively researched inhibitors capable of targeting both Aβ and tau aggregation. Renowned as a popular reagent for detecting Aβ aggregation, Congo red is often utilized due to its affinity towards β-sheet secondary structures such as those in amyloid fibrils [17]. Congo red has also been demonstrated to interfere with the Aβ misfolding and aggregation process [18,19]. Moreover, in vitro studies and investigations on caspase-cleaved tau mouse models have revealed Congo red's inhibitory effects on tau aggregation [20]. Despite these promising observations, there remains a notable gap in research investigating Congo red's dual inhibition of Aβ and tau aggregation. Furthermore, Congo red's inability to effectively cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB) significantly restrains its practicality as a viable CNS drug candidate [20]. This barrier limits direct access to the brain, reducing Congo red's therapeutic potential for neurodegenerative conditions such as AD.

Carbon dots (CD) are quasi-zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, typically characterized by an average size below 10 nm. Their unique attributes, including high biocompatibility, minimal cytotoxicity, abundant surface sites for functional groups, and exceptional photoluminescence (PL) properties [21-25], have propelled their utility across diverse biochemical applications. Their applications encompass bioimaging [25-27], biosensing [28,29], drug delivery [25,30,31], and nanomedicine [31-33]. Notably, our group recently demonstrated that CD synthesized from urea and citric acid exhibit the capability to cross the BBB and effectively inhibit tau aggregation [21,34,35]. This discovery positions CD as potential drug candidates serving dual roles as therapeutic agents and drug nanocarriers, holding promise for the treatment of AD. The selection of precursor materials significantly influences the structural and functional characteristics of CD [22,36,37]. Hence, the choice of appropriate precursors plays a pivotal role in tailoring CD for specific applications. Employing Congo red as a precursor material for CD holds the potential to enhance Congo red's ability to penetrate the BBB. Furthermore, harnessing the inherent capability of Congo red to inhibit both Aβ and tau aggregation presents a compelling and promising therapeutic avenue for addressing AD. This strategic utilization of Congo red as a precursor material for CD offers a new direction towards the development of effective therapies for AD.

In this study, we successfully synthesized three types of Congo red-derived CD (CRCD). These CRCD exhibit a unique ability to inhibit both tau and Aβ aggregation, marking a significant advancement in AD therapeutic research. Our investigation extensively explored the physicochemical properties of CRCD derived utilizing different mass ratios of precursors. A comprehensive array of analytical techniques, including Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), derivative thermogravimetry (DTG), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), mass spectrometry (MS), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), were employed to thoroughly characterize these nanomaterials. This rigorous physicochemical characterization has provided valuable insights into the unique properties of CRCD, offering a foundation for optimizing their performance in future applications. Moreover, these findings revealed that CRCD exhibit robust and sustained low cytotoxicity. Particularly, CRCD3 displayed the ability to cross the BBB in an in vivo zebrafish model, showcasing its potential for CNS applications. Most notably, our study also demonstrated that CRCD possess the ability to significantly inhibit both tau and Aβ aggregation in vitro. This dual inhibitory effect positions CRCD as promising candidates for the treatment of AD, providing an auspicious direction in the development of therapeutic interventions targeting multiple facets of AD pathology.

Citric acid (⩾98 %) and Congo red (⩾35 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The experiments utilized dialysis tubing with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 3.5 kDa, procured from Spectrum Labs, Inc. (Rancho Dominguez, CA). Deionized (DI) water served as the solvent for the experiments, acquired under stable conditions (resistivity: 18.2 MΩ.cm; pH: 6.6 ± 0.3; surface tension: 72.6 mN·m−1; at 20 °C) through the Milli-Q system. Thioflavin T (ThT) used for the ThT assays was purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Amyloid beta 1–42 peptide (Aβ42) was obtained from rPeptide (Watkinsville, GA, USA). The tau protein used was over-expressed in BL21 DE3 E. coli in an LB media and subsequently purified using liquid chromatography.

To prepare the CRCD samples, varying mass ratios of Congo red and citric acid were mixed and dissolved in 15 mL of DI water. Consistently, 1045 mg Congo red was used for all three samples, while 384, 768, and 1536 mg of citric acid were utilized for samples 1, 2 and 3, respectively. The resulting solutions underwent heating in a Muffle furnace at 180 °C for 5 hrs. After heating step, both large solid particles and CRCD were formed. The solutions were centrifuged twice at 9000 rpm and 4 °C for 30 min each, aiming to remove large solid particles. Subsequently, the solutions were filtered through a 0.2 µm filter and then dialyzed against DI water for 3 days using dialysis tubing with a MWCO of 3.5 kDa. Following this process, the resulting samples were labeled as CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 based on the varying mass ratios of Congo red and citric acid utilized in their preparation.

The Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired using an Agilent Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. For photoluminescence (PL) characterization, a HORIBA Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3 fluorometer equipped with a 5 nm slit width for both emission and excitation was employed. The PL emission spectra were normalized using OriginPro 9.1 software. Quartz cuvettes with a 1 cm optical pathlength were utilized for both UV–vis and PL characterizations. Attenuated total reflection - Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) was carried out using a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrometer equipped with a Smartorbit operation to analyze the chemical composition of the CRCD. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) measurements were performed on a high-precision thermo-microbalance (Netzsch TG 209 F3 Tarsus, Netzsch, USA) under a nitrogen atmosphere. The temperature was gradually increased from 40 to 1000 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min. The spectra for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were acquired using a Physical Electronics PHI 5000 Versaprobe scanning X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The system utilized a monochromatic Al K-alpha X-ray source with an energy of 1486.6 eV, operating at a 15 kV voltage and 1 mA anode current. For atomic force microscopy (AFM) data, CRCD aqueous solution (0.1 mg/mL) was deposited onto a silica mica slide and imaged in tapping mode using an applied force of 3 N/m with a 5420 atomic force microscope. Additionally, TEM imaging was performed using a JOEL 1200 × microscope to determine the size of CRCD particles. To ensure the reliability and accuracy, all characterization was carried out three times on independent batches of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3. This repetition allowed for validation and consistency in the obtained results across multiple samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of fluorescence quantum yield of CRCD
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the three types of CRCD, quinine sulfate and harmane were utilized as reference standards with reported QY values of 54 and 83 %, respectively [38,39]. For this assessment, the reference standards were dissolved in a solution with a refractive index (n) of 1.33, precisely 0.1 M H2SO4, while the three types of CRCD were dissolved in DI water, maintaining the same refractive index. UV–vis absorbance spectra analysis revealed minimal absorbance intensity (< 0.05) for both reference standards and the three types of CRCD at 350 nm. Following this, PL emission spectra were recorded for the reference standards and all three CRCD using an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Subsequently, average absorbance values and spectral integrated areas of PL were incorporated into the equation to derive the QY:
Φ
=
Φ
R ×
(
I / I R
)
×
(
1 − 10 − O D R / 1 − 10 − O D
)
×
(
n 2 / n R 2
)

Here, Φ represents the QY, subscript R denotes the reference standard, n is the refractive index, I means the integrated area under the PL curve, and OD signifies the optical density of samples with absorbance at 350 nm.

The HEK 293 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% of Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 1% of penicillin–streptomycin. The cells were then seeded into a 96-well plate at a density of approximately 3 × 103 cells per well. After 24 hrs of incubation at 37 °C, the cells were exposed to fresh DMEM containing varying concentrations (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, and 100 µg/mL) of CRCD1, CRCD2, CRCD3 and Congo red for 72 hrs. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with 20 µL of 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)−2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution (5 mg/mL in PBS) for 4 hrs. The supernatant was then removed, and 100 µL DMSO was added. After complete dissolution of the purple formazan crystals, the absorbance of each well was measured at 570 nm using a Molecular Devices SpectraMax i3x plate reader. Results were expressed as percentages relative to the control group (0 µg/mL). Similar procedures of the HEK 293 cell protocol were employed for the HeLa cells. Each experiment was performed in triplicate to ensure the reliability and reproducibility of the obtained results.

Zebrafish larvae, obtained from the University of Miami Zebrafish Core Facility at 5 days post fertilization of the wild-type Danio rerio, were selected for imaging. Six zebrafish larvae were selected to inject the three CRCD, respectively. To facilitate the injection process, the zebrafish larvae were anesthetized using tricaine. Subsequently, they were intravascularly injected with 100 nL aqueous solutions of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3, each at a concentration of 30 mg/mL. The injections were administered directly into the heart region of the larvae. Following the injection procedure, the zebrafish larvae were subjected to imaging using a Leica SP5 confocal microscope. The imaging protocol encompassed the utilization of both white light and excitation wavelength of 405 nm to capture the necessary data. It should be noted that this experiment was conducted in adherence to an animal care protocol approved by the University of Miami Animal Care and Use Committee. Furthermore, all procedures were performed in compliance with the guidelines established by the National Science Foundation regarding ethical handling and care of experimental animals.

All aggregation experiments were conducted on a Tecan Infinite M1000 microplate reader in a white 384-well Optiplate (Perkin Elmer LLC) at 37 °C with 250 rpm orbital shaking. Measurements were taken every 20 min for either 60 h (for his-tagged full-length tau; his-tau) or 16 h (for amyloid beta 1–42), with Thioflavin T (ThT) excited at 435 nm (λex) and emission recorded at 480 nm (λem). For each ThT experiment, a mixture of heparin (10 µM), his-tau (10 µM), ThT (10 µM) and DTT (2.5 mM) were dissolved in buffer (10 mM HEPES, 100 mM NaCl, pH 7.2) to form a master mix. Aβ42 was purchased from rPeptide. The lyophilized peptide was dissolved in hexafluoroisopropanol (HFIP) to ensure the starting reagent is monomeric following the protocol from Pavliukeviciene et al. [40], then dried under a nitrogen stream to remove remaining HFIP. The monomeric Aβ42 was dissolved in a modified master mix (10 µM ThT, 10 mM Tris, 150 mM NaCl, pH 7.5) with stirring at 4 °C to reach a final concentration of 50 µM. Each sample consisted of 120 µL of the his-tau or Aβ42 master mix and contained 2 µL of CRCD, buffer, or reactive tweezer (known inhibitor for tau aggregation). These samples were then equally distributed between four wells for a working volume of approximately 30 µL total in each well [41]. Each sample was run in quadruplicate as described and averaged to give the resulting graphs. Further data analysis was performed using Igor Pro (Wavemetrics, Lake Oswego, OR, USA) and GraphPad (GraphPad Software, Boston, MA, USA) to extract more kinetic parameters regarding aggregation. The data was fit to a sigmoid curve with the equation:
S = b a s e +
m a x
1 + exp
(
x
h a l f
−
x 1
r a t e
)

This equation was applied for both aggregation curves and allows for the calculation of the half time of aggregation (x-half), base, max, and rate where the coefficient “base” sets the S value at small x. The S value at large x is base + max where x0 sets the x value at which S is (base + max/s) and rate denotes the rate of aggregation. Smaller rates indicate faster aggregation, specifically, the slope at x = x half is max/4*rate.

Additionally, IC50 data was computed by graphing the percent of aggregation (compared with the uninhibited control) to the logarithmic value of each tested concentration. Five concentrations were tested for each sample, including 143, 14.3, 1.43, 0.143, and 0.0143 µg/mL, generating five data points for each IC50 graph. These values can be used as a method for scoring drug effectiveness, specifically inhibition.

The AFM samples were prepared on glass microscope slides with double-sided tape, onto which a thin slice of mica was placed. The mica was carefully peeled until achieving a smooth, flat surface devoid of cracks or imperfections. Subsequently, 30 µL of the post-aggregation assay samples were gently injected onto the mica. Approximately 100 µL of double distilled water (ddH2O) was utilized to spread the sample to more of the mica and ensure dilution of the salts before letting the sample dry. The sample was allowed to sit on the mica for 5–10 min, then dabbed with a chem wipe by tilting the slide to the side and dabbing only at the edge without putting the chem wipe on top of the mica and risking contamination. Finally, the slides were left in a covered Petri dish to dry, avoiding dust accumulation. For a faster dry, nitrogen could be gently blown on top.

The AFM used for imaging was an Asylum MFP3D AFM from Asylum Research, capable of accurately capturing images ranging from 90 to 1 µm (possibly smaller with less accuracy). Two magnets were used to hold the slide in position and a fresh silicon probe from Asylum Research was employed to acquire images. AFMs generally utilize sharp tipped probes at the end of flexible cantilevers to scan and record height measurements or the topography of the sample on the mica. The probe's lever was aluminum coated although there was no coating on the tip, with a radius of 7 nm. All images were acquired with a frame size of 5 by 5 µm to ensure a clear view of the morphologies that occurred. The sample acquisition was performed with scan frequencies between 1 and 4 Hz at room temperature. Some images were processed further to reduce scars or noise from vibrations or imperfections.

Samples were collected from 30 µL stocks remaining in the four separate wells described during the ThT assays of tau aggregation with or without the presence of CRCD1 and at different concentrations. The four wells were pooled to make a 120 µL stock which was used for sample preparation. Afterwards, 5 µL of the samples were placed on parafilm and the grid was placed on the sample with the plastic side touching the sample, shiny side facing out. The grids and samples were left to incubate for one minute. Next, the grids were washed with three drops of water before being stained with 2 drops of 1.5 % uranyl acetate and incubated again for another minute. Finally, the grids were washed again with a drop of water to remove excess stain and blotted gently with filter paper (at the corners). These grids, when dry, were then placed in a storage box with the samples facing to the left. The grids were inserted gently into the TEM and locked into place before beginning image acquisition. Images were collected at various magnifications, but for continuity all images in this paper were collected at a magnification of 25000X and a high voltage of 100 kV. The images also contain scale bars that mark 500 nm and the concentration of inhibitor that was used in each experiment.

The statistical analysis was performed using OriginLab Pro 9.1 software. A minimum of three independent replicates of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 were used for UV–vis absorption, PL, ATR-FTIR, TGA, XPS, AFM, TEM and MTT cell viability characterization to ensure the reliability and reproducibility. The mean ± standard deviation (STD) was used to present continuous variables of cytotoxicity measurements. XPS background was corrected using a Shirley line-shape type function.

The statistical analysis for the ThT assay data involved a two-variable, paired variance, t-test comparing the fluorescence intensity (AU) values for each CRCD at all five concentrations. The same t-test was used for the IC50 data to calculate the significance. The tests were completed using GraphPad software and Microsoft Excel software. Significance was marked in comparison with uninhibited tau aggregation with *, **, and *** meaning a p-value of ≤0.05, ≤0.01, and ≤0.001 respectively. The statistical significance of cytotoxicity results was also calculated using a t-test for 100 µg/mL condition in comparison with the 0 µg/mL control group and indicated as no significance (ns) (p-value ≥ 0.05) and ** (p-value ≤ 0.01).

In this study, water-soluble CD were synthesized utilizing Congo red and citric acid as precursors in three distinct mass ratios, employing a Muffle furnace approach. Post-synthesis, purification procedures were undertaken to eliminate any unreacted precursors and intermediates, yielding three CRCD named CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3. The UV–vis spectra of Congo red (Fig. S1A) exhibited absorption bands at 237, 343 and 497 nm, indicative of π-π* electronic transition of C = C, π-π* transition of NH and n-π* transition of N = N group, respectively [42,43]. Conversely, CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 (Fig. 1A) displayed a blue shift in absorption bands compared to Congo red, attributed to structural alternations in the synthesized CD. These CRCD presented three absorption bands: the first at 226 nm, associated with the π-π* transition of C = C; the second at 294 nm, possibly assigned to n-π* electronic transition of C = O from the structure of citric acid and/or π-π* transition of NH from the structure of Congo red; the third absorption band at 423 nm can be ascribed to the n-π* transition of N = N group. In Fig. 1B-D, under an excitation of 430 nm, all three CRCD demonstrated excitation-independent PL emission within the range of 400–600 nm, with the highest emission observed at 456 nm. In contrast, Congo red displayed an emission peak at 610 nm with the highest excitation at 470 nm (Fig. S1B). To ensure QY measurements accuracy, cross-calibration was performed using reference standards - quinine sulfate and harmane – known for exhibiting similar PL behavior to CRCD. Based on our prior research [27], setting the excitation wavelength at 350 nm for both standards and CRCD, cross-calibration determined the QY of quinine sulfate and harmane to be 56.5 % (with a literature value of Φ = 54 %) and 80.6 % (with a literature value of Φ = 83 %), respectively. The assay's reliability was confirmed by these findings. Employing the same protocol, the QY of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 were calculated, resulting in 8, 11 and 15 %, respectively with an error of ±1 %.

The FTIR spectroscopy played a crucial role in examining the functional groups within CRCD1, CRCD2, and CRCD3, comparing them against the characteristic patterns observed in citric acid and Congo red. The spectrum of citric acid (Fig. S2A) exhibited specific peak patterns. OH stretching between 3551 and 3005 cm−1 corresponded to the first peak pattern. The second pattern contained peaks at 1747 and 1698 cm−1 attributed to C = O stretching, while the third, at approximately 1377 cm −1, denoted the stretching vibration of CO bonds. Additional peaks observed between 3005 and 2031 cm−1 were linked to the CH stretching and the peak at 1157 cm−1 were assigned to OH bending. Examining the spectrum of Congo red (Fig. S2B) revealed distinctive peaks, including the asymmetrical stretching of NH at 3465 cm−1 and CH stretching in a benzene ring at 3077 cm−1 [44]. Peaks at 1594, 1445, and 1356 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching of an NH bond, C = C stretching in benzene, and the stretching vibration of a CN bond, respectively. The peaks at 1184 and 1045 cm−1 were attributed to the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching of S = O [44]. In comparison, the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 ) of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 showed a broad band at 3404 cm−1, indicating NH and/or OH stretching. A new peak at 1713 cm−1 emerged, possibly due to the stretching of a C = O bond arising from the reaction between Congo red and citric acid, leading to the synthesis of CD. The peak at 1630 cm−1 was attributed to C = O stretching and/or NH bending, whereas the peaks at 1380 cm−1 indicated CO and/or CN stretching. Moreover, the 1495 cm−1 peak corresponded to the C = C stretching in benzene. Peaks at 1181 and 1047 cm−1 were associated with the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching of S = O, confirming the presence of both C = O and S = O bonds in the synthesized CRCD. These observations further support the occurrence of a reaction between Congo red and citric acid, resulting in the formation of CRCD with distinct chemical properties.

TGA and DTG measurements were conducted to identify and quantify the functional moieties based on their distinct decomposition temperatures. The TGA and DTG results of CRCD1, CRCD2, CRCD3, Congo red, and citric acid were presented in Fig. 3 and Fig. S3. Comparative analysis of CRCD and Congo red, in contrast to citric acid, revealed a similar thermal decomposition trend, indicating the presence of the Congo red within CRCD. The varied mass losses also emphasized the significant role played by citric acid in the structural formation of CRCD. Notably, residual elements observed after the decomposition of Congo red and CRCD were likely Na2SO3 or Na2SO4, owing to the presence of Na+ and -SO3 2- in Congo red. This was corroborated by a reduction in residue mass with an increasing amount of citric acid during the CRCD synthesis process. Furthermore, DTG measurements (Fig. 3D-F and Fig. S3B) highlighted structural differences between CRCD and the two precursor materials, summarized as follows: (1) Congo red exhibited 5 decomposition stages, ranging from 40 to 1000 °C: 40–199, 199–320, 320–458, 458–650 and 650–1000 °C. At the initial stage (40–199 °C), the observed was attributed to the desorption of water molecules from Congo red. Typically, the loss of water can result from moisture and/or intramolecular dehydration condensation reactions. Because Congo red lacks hydroxyl groups, the mass loss of Congo red is hypothesized to be primarily via moisture desorption. The stage at 199–320 °C was associated with the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups and sublimation of small carbon frameworks, where no mass loss occurred, indicating the non-decomposition of -SO3 − during this stage. Following this, the stage at 320–458 °C indicated the decomposition of amines and the most mass loss observed was at 458–650 °C which can be assigned to the decomposition of an aromatic structure within Congo red. Finally, the last stage (650–1000 °C) indicated the mass loss associated with the remaining cyclic structure. (2) The TGA and DTG of citric acid have been reported in our earlier research [34], demonstrated by two decomposition stages: 150–300 and 300–600 °C. In the first stage, the TGA of citric acid showed the loss of water molecules adsorbed and formed by intramolecular dehydration condensation. Within this temperature range, citric acid undergoes transformation into aconitic acid, itaconic acid and anhydrous itaconic acid, releasing CO2 and water molecules. The subsequent stage (300–600 °C) exhibited mass loss attributed to the decomposition of relatively stable oxygen-containing functional groups and the sublimation of small carbon frameworks. (3) The three different CRCD each presented four decomposition stages, as summarized in Table S1. In the initial stage, TGA analysis of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 displayed similar mass losses attributed to adsorbed water and intramolecular dehydration condensation. The subsequent stage revealed varying mass losses among the different CRCD, primarily related to the decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups. The mass losses of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 were measured at 11.87, 13.17 and 18.25%, respectively, suggesting an increased reaction between citric acid and Congo red in the synthesis process as the amount of citric acid increased. The third stage illustrated a mass reduction of 64.89, 63.05 and 49.97% for CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3, respectively, indicated the decomposition of amines and aromatic structures. Finally, the fourth stage represented the mass loss of the remaining cyclic structures. CRCD1 and CRCD2 exhibited mass loss of 3.80 and 2.72%, respectively, whereas CRCD3 showed a mass loss of 17.83 %, suggesting a higher presence of cyclic structure in CRCD3. It is worth noting that the TGA of CRCD3 significantly differed from that of CRCD1 and CRCD2 in these stages, which means the structure of CRCD3 is different from the other synthesized CRCD.

The structural analysis of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 was further obtained through XPS spectra, displayed in Fig. S4. Elemental distribution details are summarized in Table S2, while highly resolved spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and S 2p are presented in Fig. 4 . Table S3 describes the distributions of functionalities. Elemental compositions (Table S2) revealed a decrease in carbon percentage from around 67 % for CRCD1 and CRCD2 to approximately 60 % for CRCD3. This decline was accompanied by a reduction in nitrogen and sulfur content and an increase in oxygen. This trend likely resulted from the increased amount of citric acid, which considerably reduced the heteroatom amount originating solely from Congo red. In Table S3 and Fig. 4A-C, all three samples showed the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon (284.7 eV), accounting for 73, 68 and 64 % in CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3, respectively. No C = X related signals were detected (X: N, S). while C-X (X: O, N, S) signal were evident in all samples, with percentages rising from CRCD1 to CRCD3. Similarly, carboxylic functionalities followed this trend. The O 1 s spectra (Fig. 4D-F) showed a consistent increment of carboxylic signal (535.5 eV) alongside a decrement in both CO (530.5 eV) and S-O (536.8 eV) signals with increasing citric acid content. Furthermore, the S 2p spectra reported in Fig. 4J-L revealed a decline in sulphonate residues (169.4 eV) and a simultaneous increase in sulphoxide species (168.2 eV) up to 79 % for CRCD3. The formation of sulphone species could also promote UV–vis absorption, compensating for the loss of unsaturated NN bonds. N1s spectra (Fig. 4G-I) exhibited a drastic decrease in amino residues (399.1 eV) from 69 % in CRCD1 to 18 % in CRCD3. Pyridinic nitrogen (N6, 398.2 eV) and graphitic nitrogen (NQ, 400.1 eV) increased with the rising citric acid content, reaching 16 % and 66 %, respectively, in CRCD3.

MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry was used to analyze Congo red, CRCD1, CRCD2, and CRCD3. The mass spectra of the three CRCD (Fig. 5 ) all exhibit two main mass ion peaks at m/z = 506 and 593 with an additional mass ion peak at m/z = 439 corresponding to a fraction of Congo red. Fig. S5 shows two fractions of Congo red with mass ion peaks at m/z = 255 and 441 which suggests the breakdown of Congo red and the interaction between Congo red and citric acid. We further developed a hypothesis for the synthesis mechanism of the three CRCD (Fig. S6) based on the results of XPS, FTIR, TGA, and Mass spectrometry. Most sodium ions are replaced by hydrogen after the introduction of citric acid and Congo red breaks into molecules B and C. Citric acid reacts with the amine groups in molecule C to form molecules E and F, which have the same molecular weight (592 g/mol) with mass ion peaks at m/z = 593 (Fig. 5). Amide bonds are formed during this reaction, accompanied by the loss of H2O. With an increase in citric acid, cyclic anhydrides formed with a molecular weight around 504 g/mol, which aligns with the increase in the intensity of mass ion peaks at m/z = 506 from molecules D and E.

The XPS spectra of CRCD provided insights into the structures of the analyzed species, revealing that the Congo red scaffold drove the condensation process and induced the formation of highly functionalized, condensed, heteroaromatic structures. NQ species were detected only within aromatic planes, while N6 and sulphone species may be exclusively present on the edges of the aromatic structures. Considering all analyses from TGA and FT-IR, we proposed a model illustrating the structure of CRCD: (i) the absence of C = X (X = N, S), (ii) the reduction of sulphonate and amino residues, and (iii) the formation of condensed structures. The mass spectra and reaction mechanism supported the molecular weight and structure of each layer. For CRCD1, the carbon layers primarily result from the reaction between part C and citric acid. With an increased amount of citric acid, CRCD2 and CRCD3 exhibit two types of layers formed by different reaction methods, as depicted in Fig. S6. The model of CRCD proposed in Fig. S7 illustrates heteroatomic-doped carbon layers as the primary structure, also constituting the "core" of CRCD. Amine, oxygen, and sulfur-based functionalities reside on the surface of the CRCD, forming the "shell" and are available for further modifications.

AFM (Fig. 6 ) and TEM (Fig. S8) images were collected to investigate the morphology and size distribution of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3. To prevent self-aggregation of the CRCD, the samples were sonicated for 30 min prior to imaging. TEM analysis was employed to determine the CD homogeneity in the x-y plane, which provides a highly precise estimation of their uniformity, while AFM was utilized to measure particle sizes along the z-direction. The AFM images showed that CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 have a narrow size distribution with a mean height of 2.60, 4.34 and 6.57 nm, respectively. This reveals that the sizes of CRCD along the z-direction increased with higher quantities of citric acid used in synthesis. By measuring over 800 dots, the TEM images demonstrated a Schulz-Flory distribution within the 1–10 nm range. The data also suggested the average sizes of CRCD1, CRCD2, and CRCD3 to be approximately 2.7, 3.1, and 3.9 nm, respectively. Both AFM and TEM consistently indicated that larger quantities of citric acid resulted in an overall increase in the CD size.

The MTT assay was a pivotal tool used to meticulously evaluate the potential in vitro cytotoxicity of CRCD, by studying their impact on both HeLa and HEK 293 cells over a 72-hr incubation period. Resulting data portrayed in Fig. 7 , exhibited consistent trends for various concentrations of CRCD spanning from 6.25 to 100 µg/mL for both HeLa and HEK293 cells. Remarkably, the three types of CRCD exhibited a robust and sustained low cytotoxicity profile for HeLa cells (Fig. 7A), maintaining cell viability levels well above 90%. Even when tested on HEK 293 cells (Fig. 7B), CRCD1 displayed approximately 70 % cell viability at the highest concentration of 100 µg/mL. In contrast, CRCD2 and CRCD3 demonstrated negligible cytotoxicity effects, preserving cell viability even at their maximum concentrations. These compelling findings underscore the substantial biocompatibility and safety of the synthesized CRCD, positioning them as promising and reliable candidates for applications in nanodrug delivery systems and nanocarrier technology within the expansive field of biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tau and amyloid-β aggregation inhibited by CRCD
ce:section.ce:para: Previously published research has shown that Congo red can interfere with the Aβ oligomerization and aggregation process [18,19], and marginally inhibit tau aggregation in vitro [20]. However, it's crucial to note that there hasn't been any previous indication of Congo red serving as a dual inhibitor for both Aβ and tau aggregation simultaneously. Moreover, in its unmodified form, Congo red is unable to traverse the BBB, making it unfit to be used as a drug for neurodegenerative diseases [45]. Building upon this noteworthy finding, we propose a hypothesis that CRCD, derived from Congo red, may preserve the capability to inhibit both tau and Aβ42 aggregation. This hypothesis presents an innovative perspective and opens doors for further exploration into the therapeutic potential of CRCD in combatting neurodegenerative diseases.

We demonstrated that CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 can effectively inhibit tau aggregation in a dose-dependent manner, a key observation shown in Fig. S9. Thioflavin T (ThT), known for its strong fluorescence when bound to the stacked β-sheet conformation in tau aggregates and amyloid fibrils, served as an efficient indicator of aggregation [46]. Unlike Thioflavin S and other aggregation indicators, ThT experiences a ∼40 nm shift in fluorescence emission when bound to amyloid fibrils, allowing for accurate quantifications of the observed aggregation [47]. In the tau aggregation assays utilizing ThT, a notable maximum fluorescence of approximately 16,500 arbitrary units (AU) was recorded, indicating significant binding of ThT to the fibrils formed by tau. Upon the addition of CRCD1, tau aggregation was significantly inhibited at concentrations as low as 0.143 µg/mL, with a maximum fluorescence at ∼9,500AU, and completely inhibited with a CRCD concentration at 14.3 µg/mL, as indicated by the decline in ThT fluorescence intensity (Fig. S9A). This inhibitory effect can be seen for all three CRCD, (Fig. 8 A and S9). Further analysis of the ThT data collected demonstrated essential parameters for assessing tau aggregation: a half-time of 24.47 ± 0.15 h for tau aggregation, a rate of aggregation at 9.05 ± 0.13 hrs−1, a lag time estimated at 6.36 ± 0.12 hrs, and a plateau reached at approximately 16,455 ± 130 AU.

Consistent with the above hypothesis, our findings indicate that all three CRCD also effectively inhibit Aβ42 aggregation, as monitored by ThT assays in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 8B and S10). Mirroring the trends observed in tau aggregation, Fig. S10A displayed that CRCD1 significantly hindered Aβ42 aggregation at a concentration of 1.43 µg/mL, achieving complete inhibition at 143 µg/mL, notably a higher concentration than was required for tau. This inhibitory effect was consistently demonstrated across all three CRCD, with CRCD3 exhibiting dual inhibitory effects on Aβ42 aggregation in addition to its ability to penetrate the BBB (Fig. 8B). For Aβ42 aggregation, an analysis of the ThT curves also revealed specific parameters: a half-time of 4.76 ± 0.04 hrs for aggregation, a rate of aggregation at 1.82 ± 0.01 hrs−1, a lag time estimated at 1.13 ± 0.02 hrs, and a plateau at approximately 12,574 ± 337 AU. These values indicate a faster aggregation process for Aβ42 in comparison to tau, but also a lower plateau for uninhibited aggregation.

The quantification of tau aggregation through ThT assays was further validated by visualizing the aggregates using AFM (Fig. 9 ). This comprehensive analysis involved imaging tau aggregates at varying concentrations of CRCD1 - highest (143 µg/mL), mid (1.43 µg/mL), and lowest concentrations (0.0143 µg/mL) - demonstrating an obvious decline in the quantity of observed tau aggregates. The fibrillar morphology of tau aggregates can be abundantly seen in uninhibited samples and are consistent with past literature [48]. As the concentration of CRCD increases, the fibrillar aggregates disappear. Interestingly, the AFM images showing a concentration of 143 µg/mL for CRCD1 alongside tau samples displayed mostly CRCD1 particles with minimal tau fibrils present. This evident reduction in tau fibrils captured by AFM reinforces and corroborates the findings obtained through ThT fluorescence assays, validating the inhibitory effect of CRCD1 on tau aggregation. This dual confirmation using both ThT assays and AFM imaging further solidifies the efficacy of CRCD in mitigating tau aggregation.

Additionally, TEM was employed (Fig. 10 ) to complement the observations obtained through AFM visualization and to validate the findings garnered using ThT assays. The data includes two images for each of the four concentrations under study. The four concentrations shown are the same as those analyzed using AFM, these include 143 (240 µM), 1.43 (2.4 µM), 0.0143 µg/mL (0.024 µM), and no CRCD1. These results demonstrate a visible increase in tau protein aggregation as the concentration of CRCD1 decreases, corroborating its significant inhibitory effect, as suggested by ThT data collection. These TEM images not only align with the AFM findings demonstrating the aggregation of tau fibrils, but also substantiate the conclusion drawn from ThT assay analysis, asserting that CRCD1 serves as an effective inhibitor of such aggregation. The consistency across these diverse methodologies further reinforces the efficacy of CRCD in impeding tau protein aggregation.

In addition, the IC50 values for the three inhibitors were calculated by fitting a sigmoidal function to the inhibition percentage values plotted against the corresponding inhibitor concentrations on a semi-logarithmic scale. These values demonstrate the concentration of inhibitors necessary to inhibit 50 % of possible aggregation. Shown in Fig. 11 were the calculated IC50 values for tau (Fig. 11A) and Aβ42 (Fig. 11B), which notably illustrate the highly effective nature of CRCD against both neuropathogenic proteins, showcasing a distinctive bifunctional feature. Specifically, CRCD1 represented the lowest IC50 value for tau inhibition (∼0.2 ± 0.1 µg/mL) and Aβ42 inhibition (∼2.1 ± 0.5 µg/mL) compared to CRCD2 and CRCD3. The molar IC50 values for CRCD1, CRCD2, and CRCD3 were calculated to be ∼0.3, ∼1.2, and ∼2.5 µM respectively for tau and ∼3.5, ∼5.2, and ∼10.6 µM respectively for Aβ aggregation. These outcomes solidify the position of CRCD1 as the most potent dual inhibitor against both tau and Aβ aggregation, but also suggest the effectiveness of CRCD3 to inhibit both aggregated hallmarks of AD.

Congo red has been known to bind to β-sheets, characteristics of amyloid aggregation, through a combination of hydrophobic interactions and electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged sulfate groups of Congo red and the positively charged amino acid residues of the protofilaments [45]. After binding, Congo red aligns in parallel with the β-sheet side chains while orienting perpendicular to the fibril direction [49]. The protofilaments arrange themselves in such a pattern that the side chains on both sides of the β-sheet form orderly rows running along the direction of the sheets, which are perpendicular to the strands [50]. The resulting gaps between every second residue creates openings for the insertion of Congo red [45,51]. Notably, the structure of Congo red features symmetrically anti-parallel β-strands that fit into the gaps [52]. Aβ aggregates have a secondary structure that consists primarily of β-sheets unless they are being unraveled or inhibited somehow [53-55]. The binding between Congo red and the β-sheets of both Aβ and tau stabilizes native monomers or partially folded intermediates, thus inhibiting fibril formation and reducing the concentration of oligomers [45]. Based on the previous work demonstrating Congo red's mechanism for interfering in β-sheet aggregates, we have also proposed a simplified schematic interpretation of how CRCD interfere with β-sheet aggregations, as shown in Fig. 12 . Our findings suggest that CRCD retain most structural characteristics of Congo red even with the introduction of citric acid during the synthesis process. Thus, CRCD can potentially possess similar abilities to inhibit aggregates. CRCD1 exhibits superiority in inhibitory effects when compared with CRCD2 and CRCD3, a feature that might be attributed to its smallest particle size and high Congo red density. With a smaller size, CRCD1 offers a larger surface area coverage, providing an increased number of active binding sites for interaction between CRCD and Aβ or tau, thereby leading to stronger inhibition of both aggregates. Moreover, the superior inhibitory effect of CRCD1 could be due to its higher mass ratio of Congo red when compared with the other two types of CRCD. The different inhibitory effects of CRCD for Aβ and tau may stem from the distinct protein interaction interfaces between the CRCD and the proteins [45]. Even though CRCD1 is the most effective dual inhibitor for both tau and Aβ aggregation, its inability to efficiently cross the BBB limits its potential use for the treatment of brain diseases. Prior research showed that black carbon dots obtained the capability to cross the BBB upon conjugating with transferrin [21]. To address this limitation, our future endeavors aim to conjugate CRCD1 with other molecules, such as transferrin, to enhance its ability to cross the BBB, thereby broadening its applicability in related CNS diseases.

In our previous investigations [21], we revealed the traversing ability of carbon nitride dots and gel-like CD through the BBB, successfully reaching the central canal of the spinal cord in zebrafish. Our current study aims to ascertain whether CRCD possess similar traits and exhibit the capacity to cross the BBB. Zebrafish were used as an in vivo model to comprehensively evaluate and compare the abilities of CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 to penetrate the BBB. Both mice and zebrafish are frequently used as in vivo models for BBB-related studies. Zebrafish stand as an ideal choice for our experiments due to various advantageous characteristics when compared to other models like mice. Zebrafish provide a prolific number of offspring, allowing for extended and larger-scale research, while also being cost-effective and requiring less space for housing. Moreover, the transparent embryos and larvae facilitate convenient observation of fluorescently labeled activities within the bloodstream and brain. Remarkably, zebrafish possess a CNS physiology that closely mimics that of humans, including neurotransmitters, hormones, and receptors [27]. These collective advantages significantly influenced our decision to utilize zebrafish as the preferred in vivo model for this work.

CRCD1, CRCD2 and CRCD3 aqueous solutions, each with a concentration of 30 mg/mL, were prepared separately and intravascularly injected into the heart of wild-type zebrafish larvae, 5 days post-fertilization. Notably, the confocal imaging results displayed in Fig. 13 revealed a blue photoluminescence specifically in the central canal of the spinal cord, illustrating the presence of CRCD3 within the CNS. In stark contrast, there was no discernible signal detected for CRCD1 and CRCD2 within the CNS, establishing an inability to penetrate the BBB despite their smaller size. The BBB's tight junctions between endothelial cells maintain a spacing of 4–6 nm [21,56], yet the inability of CRCD1 and CRCD2 to cross the BBB suggests that size may not be the sole determinant in this study. Amphiphilicity is another factor enabling CD to pass through the BBB via passive diffusion. The BBB contains a hydrophobic lipid barrier, hence upon entering the BBB, molecules must transition to a hydrophilic state to continue to move through the brain fluid within the CNS [56]. Among the three different CRCD, CRCD3 has the greatest quantity of citric acid as a precursor, which has presumably enhanced its amphiphilicity. This augmented amphiphilicity could account for CRCD3’s successful traversal of the BBB, enabling its presence in the CNS. The ability of CRCD3 to overcome the BBB barrier represents its potential as a nanocarrier for drug delivery applications targeting brain-related disorders and diseases. This breakthrough offers promising prospects for utilizing Congo red-derived CD as effective vehicles for delivering therapeutics to the brain.

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the leading form of senile dementia, ranking third among the most prevalent causes of mortality in elderly populations (pre-COVID-19), following heart disease and cancer. Despite several AD drugs being approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), there remains a scarcity of effective treatments that modify the progression of the disease. This study demonstrates the potential of Congo red-derived CD (CRCD) as innovative nanomaterials in inhibiting both amyloid-beta (Aβ) and tau aggregation, a feat that has not yet been accomplished using a single previously synthesized type of carbon dots (CD). The newly synthesized CRCD not only exhibited a robust and sustained low cytotoxicity but also displayed superior inhibitory effects against both Aβ and tau aggregation compared to previously reported CD. Specifically, CRCD3 emerged as a noteworthy candidate, showcasing the capability for dual inhibition of Aβ and tau aggregation while efficiently crossing the blood-brain barrier (BBB) in a zebrafish model. This attribute positions CRCD3 as a promising contender for drug delivery systems targeting neurodegenerative diseases. These findings highlight the significant improvements in inhibitory efficacy and BBB penetration achieved through CRCD synthesis, laying a crucial groundwork for future therapeutic developments in AD and other neurodegenerative conditions. Future investigations should concentrate on elucidating the direct mechanisms that underlie the observed inhibitory effects and evaluating the efficacy of CRCD in AD mouse models to gain a comprehensive understanding of their practical implications in therapeutic intervention.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statement of the use of animals
ce:section.ce:para: This animal experiment was conducted in adherence to an animal care protocol approved by the University of Miami Animal Care and Use Committee. All procedures were performed in compliance with the guidelines established by the National Science Foundation regrading ethical handing and care of experimental animals.
